devanagari: अथ प्रथमोऽध्यायः 12. ॥ 1 ॥
english_devnagari: atha prathamo
translation:
King Parīkṣit requested:
When Śrī Kṛṣṇa, the ornament of the race of Yadus, retired to His glorious abode (in Vaikuṇṭha), whose dynasty- continued to rule over the earth? Be pleased to narrate that to me, O sage.

devanagari: प्रद्योतसंज्ञं राजानं कर्ता यत्पालकः सुतः । विशाखयूपस्तत्पुत्रो भविता राजकस्ततः ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: pradyotasaṃjñaṃ rājānaṃ kartā yatpālakaḥ sutaḥ viśākhayūpastatputro bhavitā rājakastataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
O king, Purañjaya[1], who has been named as the last prince in Bṛhadratha’s dynasty, is yet to be born. His minister Śunaka will, however, assassinate his master Purañjaya and will install his son Pradyota[2], as the king on his throne. His son will be pālaka whose successor will be Viśākhayūpa from whom will be born Rājaka.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Bh, P. IX.22.49 refers to him as Ripuñjaya.

Footnote [2]:
Historically Pradyotas did not rule Magadha. Buddhist records and Purāṇic accounts make Caṇḍa Pradyota, a contemporary of Bimbisāra, as the founder of a dynasty in Avantī and not in Magadha. It is possible that Ripuñjaya might have been treacherously murdered by his minister but he was succeeded by Bimbisāra, the founder of the Magadhan empire: BVB’S Vedic Age, P. 328

devanagari: नन्दिवर्धनस्तत्पुत्रः पञ्च प्रद्योतना इमे । अष्टत्रिंशोत्तरशतं भोक्ष्यन्ति पृथिवीं नृपाः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: nandivardhanastatputraḥ pañca pradyotanā ime aṣṭatriṃśottaraśataṃ bhokṣyanti pṛthivīṃ nṛpāḥ
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
O king, Purañjaya[1], who has been named as the last prince in Bṛhadratha’s dynasty, is yet to be born. His minister Śunaka will, however, assassinate his master Purañjaya and will install his son Pradyota[2], as the king on his throne. His son will be pālaka whose successor will be Viśākhayūpa from whom will be born Rājaka.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Bh, P. IX.22.49 refers to him as Ripuñjaya.

Footnote [2]:
Historically Pradyotas did not rule Magadha. Buddhist records and Purāṇic accounts make Caṇḍa Pradyota, a contemporary of Bimbisāra, as the founder of a dynasty in Avantī and not in Magadha. It is possible that Ripuñjaya might have been treacherously murdered by his minister but he was succeeded by Bimbisāra, the founder of the Magadhan empire: BVB’S Vedic Age, P. 328

devanagari: शिशुनागस्ततो भाव्यः काकवर्णस्तु तत्सुतः । क्षेमधर्मा तस्य सुतः क्षेत्रज्ञः क्षेमधर्मजः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: śiśunāgastato bhāvyaḥ kākavarṇastu tatsutaḥ kṣemadharmā tasya sutaḥ kṣetrajñaḥ kṣemadharmajaḥ
translation: Rājaka’s son will be Nandivardhana. These five Pradyotas will enjoy the (sovereignty of the) earth for one hundred and thirty eight years.[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
The list of Kings of the Pradyota dynasty differs considerably from Pargiter’s in The Purāṇa Text of the Dynasties of the Kali Age.
2ndly, The Pradyotas did not supplant the Bārhadrathas & rule over Magadha! The Śiśunāgas did it and it was Bimbisāra who founded the Śiśunāga dynasty and not Śiśunāga. The Pradyotas ruled at Avanti. Caṇḍa Pradyota, Udayana, Bimbisāra Ajātaśatru were in a way contemporaries of Mahāvīra & the Buddha and the historical information available shows that Pradyotas did not intervene the Bṛhadratha & Śiśunāga dynasties of Magadha.

devanagari: विधिसारः सुतस्तस्या जातशत्रुर्भविष्यति । दर्भकस्तत्सुतो भावी दर्भकस्याजयः स्मृतः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: vidhisāraḥ sutastasyā jātaśatrurbhaviṣyati darbhakastatsuto bhāvī darbhakasyājayaḥ smṛtaḥ

translation: Then there will be born Śiśunāga whose son will be Kākavarṇa. His son will be Kṣemadharmā. He will beget a son called Kṣetrajña.

devanagari: नन्दिवर्धन आजेयो महानन्दिः सुतस्ततः । शिशुनागा दशैवैते सष्ट्युत्तरशतत्रयम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: nandivardhana ājeyo mahānandiḥ sutastataḥ śiśunāgā daśaivaite saṣṭyuttaraśatatrayam
translation: He son will be Vidhisāra who will have Ajātaśatru as his son. His son will be Darbhaka while the future son of Darbhaka is Ajaya.

devanagari: समा भोक्ष्यन्ति पृथिवीं कुरुश्रेष्ठ कलौ नृपाः । महानन्दिसुतो राजन्शूद्रा गर्भोद्भवो बली ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: samā bhokṣyanti pṛthivīṃ kuruśreṣṭha kalau nṛpāḥ mahānandisuto rājanśūdrā garbhodbhavo balī

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]

translation: Nandivardhana will be the son of Ajaya, while Mahānandi will be the son of Nandivardhana. In this way, there will be ten kings from the Śiśunāga dynasty which will rule (Magadha) for three hundred and sixty years.[4]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
There are some similarities in the names of the descendants of Śiśunāgas as given in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa and Pargiter’s Purāṇa Text List. But instead of Bimbisāra, being the historical founder of the Śiśunāga dynasty, he is represented as the 5th & Ajātaśatru as the 6th descendant of Śiśunāga in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa

devanagari: महापद्मपतिः कश्चिन्नन्दः क्षत्रविनाशकृत् । ततो नृपा भविष्यन्ति शूद्र प्रायास्त्वधार्मिकाः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: mahāpadmapatiḥ kaścinnandaḥ kṣatravināśakṛt tato nṛpā bhaviṣyanti śūdra prāyāstvadhārmikāḥ
translation: O mighty Kuru, in the Kali age, they will enjoy the earth to that much period of time. The son of Mahānandi, a mighty king, will be born from the womb of a Śūdra woman.

devanagari: स एकच्छत्रां पृथिवीमनुल्लङ्घितशासनः । शासिष्यति महापद्मो द्वितीय इव भार्गवः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: sa ekacchatrāṃ pṛthivīmanullaṅghitaśāsanaḥ śāsiṣyati mahāpadmo dvitīya iva bhārgavaḥ

translation: He will be a certain Nanda, the master of a mahāpadma (treasury) and an exterminator of Kṣatriyas. After him, all kings will be as bad as Śūdras and irreligious.

devanagari: तस्य चाष्टौ भविष्यन्ति सुमाल्यप्रमुखाः सुताः । य इमां भोक्ष्यन्ति महीं राजानश्च शतं समाः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: tasya cāṣṭau bhaviṣyanti sumālyapramukhāḥ sutāḥ ya imāṃ bhokṣyanti mahīṃ rājānaśca śataṃ samāḥ

translation: Like another Paraśurāma[5], the Scion of the Bhṛgu race, Mahāpadma will rule over the entire earth under one sceptre (or royal umbrella) and his command will never be violated by any one.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
The apparent similarity between Mahāpadma and Paraśurāma is limited to putting an end to the dominance of traditional Kṣattriya families. Mahāpadma, perhaps being insulted by blue-blooded ancient Kṣattriya Kings because of his birth from a Śūdra woman, seems to have made it his policy to eliminate the dominance of these ancient Kṣattriya rulers. Hence this apparent similarity. The account of Mahāpadma as recorded by the Greek author Curtius makes him the son of a barber. Jainas (Pariśiṣṭaparvan Āvaśyaka-Sūtra) confirm Curtius. The Buddhist texts (Mahāvaṃsaṭīkā) regard Nandas as of ‘un-known lineage’ (aññāta-kula). This unanimous tradition confirms the low origin of Mahāpadma—which is a title meaning ‘a lord of infinite army’ or ‘of immense wealth? His real name is Ugrasena according to the Mahābodhivaṃśa (and it is confirmed by Greeks).

devanagari: नव नन्दान्द्विजः कश्चित्प्रपन्नानुद्धरिष्यति । तेषां अभावे जगतीं मौर्या भोक्ष्यन्ति वै कलौ ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: nava nandāndvijaḥ kaścitprapannānuddhariṣyati teṣāṃ abhāve jagatīṃ mauryā bhokṣyanti vai kalau

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: It is traditionally reported that Mahāpadma will have eight sons of whom Sumālya was prominent. These kings will enjoy this earth for one hundred years.

devanagari: स एव चन्द्र गुप्तं वै द्विजो राज्येऽभिषेक्ष्यति । तत्सुतो वारिसारस्तु ततश्चाशोकवर्धनः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: sa eva candra guptaṃ vai dvijo rājye tatsuto vārisārastu tataścāśokavardhanaḥ

translation: A certain Brāhmaṇa[6] on whom the nine Nandas were completely dependent (and practically at his mercy) will uproot them. After their extermination, the Mauryas[7] will enjoy the sovereignty of the earth in the Kali age.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā names him as Kauṭilya, Vātsyāyana or Cāṇakya. But Cāṇakya or Viṣṇugupta who uprooted that dynasty was their sworn enemy and he outwitted and out-manoeuvered Rākṣasa and destroyed Nandas. Can this be Rākṣasa, the trusted but gullible minister of Nandas (vide Mudrā-Rākṣasa) on whom Nandas depended completely.

Footnote [7]:
Padaratnāvalī is obviously quoting a wrong tradition that Mauryas were the descendants of Murā and belonged to the low caste of fishermen. Now it is accepted that Candragupta belonged to the Kṣattriya class of Moriyas ruling over Pipphalivana (Probably in U.P.)—BVB’s The Age of Imperial Unity, pp. 17 and 56.

devanagari: सुयशा भविता तस्य सङ्गतः सुयशःसुतः । शालिशूकस्ततस्तस्य सोमशर्मा भविष्यति । शतधन्वा ततस्तस्य भविता तद्बृहद्रथः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: suyaśā bhavitā tasya saṅgataḥ suyaśaḥsutaḥ śāliśūkastatastasya somaśarmā bhaviṣyati śatadhanvā tatastasya bhavitā tadbṛhadrathaḥ
translation: That very Brāhmaṇa, it is reported, will crown Candragupta. His son will be Vārisāra whose son will be Aśokavardhana.

devanagari: मौर्या ह्येते दश नृपाः सप्तत्रिंशच्छतोत्तरम् । समा भोक्ष्यन्ति पृथिवीं कलौ कुरुकुलोद्वह ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: mauryā hyete daśa nṛpāḥ saptatriṃśacchatottaram samā bhokṣyanti pṛthivīṃ kalau kurukulodvaha
translation: Aśokavardhana’s son will be Suyaśas who will beget Saṅgata. From Saṅgata will be born Śāliśuka whose son was Somaśarmā.

devanagari: अग्निमित्रस्ततस्तस्मात्सुज्येष्ठो भविता ततः । वसुमित्रो भद्रकश्च पुलिन्दो भविता सुतः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: agnimitrastatastasmātsujyeṣṭho bhavitā tataḥ vasumitro bhadrakaśca pulindo bhavitā sutaḥ
translation: From his loins will be born Śatadhanvan and his son will be Bṛhadratha. Thus there will be these ten Maurya kings[8] who will enjoy the kingdom of this world for one hundred thirty seven years.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
According to Viṣṇu Purāṇa 4.24.28-32 the Mauryan kings arc: (1) Candragupta, (2) Bindusāra, (3) Aśokavardhana, (4) Suyaśas (5) Daśaratha (not mentioned in Bh. P.) (6) Saṃyuta (7) Śāliśuka (8) Somaśarmā, (9) Śatadhanvā (10) Bṛhadratha. Pargiter’s Purāṇa Text list mentioned above shows much discrepancy about the number, names and order of the Mauryan kings; Bhāgavata Purāṇa agrees with the combined versions of Matsya and Vāyu Purāṇas. Out of these different names of Aśoka’s successors, the reality of Daśaratha is established by his three inscriptions on Nāgārjunī hills. Jain texts treat Samprati (mentioned in Matsya Purāṇa.) as a patron of Jainism as Aśoka was that of Buddhism. King Śāliśuka is mentioned in the Gārgī-Saṃhitā. No Purāṇa or Buddhist source claims Candragupta, the founder of the dynasty, as Jaina (even a convert). The final coup in 187 B.C. managed by Puṣya- mitra ended the Maurya Rule—(For details vide the Age of Imperial Unity).

devanagari: ततो घोषः सुतस्तस्माद्वज्रमित्रो भविष्यति । ततो भागवतस्तस्माद्देवभूतिः कुरूद्वह ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: tato ghoṣaḥ sutastasmādvajramitro bhaviṣyati tato bhāgavatastasmāddevabhūtiḥ kurūdvaha
translation:
From his loins will be born Śatadhanvan and his son will be Bṛhadratha. Thus there will be these ten Maurya kings[8] who will enjoy the kingdom of this world for one hundred thirty seven years.
16(A). (Having killed the Maurya king Bṛhadratha, his commander-in-chief of the army Puṣyamitra of Śuṅga race[9] will be the ruler himself). Puṣya mitra’s son will be Agnimitra to whom Sujyeṣṭha will be born.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
According to Viṣṇu Purāṇa 4.24.28-32 the Mauryan kings arc: (1) Candragupta, (2) Bindusāra, (3) Aśokavardhana, (4) Suyaśas (5) Daśaratha (not mentioned in Bh. P.) (6) Saṃyuta (7) Śāliśuka (8) Somaśarmā, (9) Śatadhanvā (10) Bṛhadratha. Pargiter’s Purāṇa Text list mentioned above shows much discrepancy about the number, names and order of the Mauryan kings; Bhāgavata Purāṇa agrees with the combined versions of Matsya and Vāyu Purāṇas. Out of these different names of Aśoka’s successors, the reality of Daśaratha is established by his three inscriptions on Nāgārjunī hills. Jain texts treat Samprati (mentioned in Matsya Purāṇa.) as a patron of Jainism as Aśoka was that of Buddhism. King Śāliśuka is mentioned in the Gārgī-Saṃhitā. No Purāṇa or Buddhist source claims Candragupta, the founder of the dynasty, as Jaina (even a convert). The final coup in 187 B.C. managed by Puṣya- mitra ended the Maurya Rule—(For details vide the Age of Imperial Unity).

Footnote [9]:
Not in SR’s text. Strangely enough Padaratnāvalī thinks Śuṅga as the proper name and Puṣyamitra his caste.

devanagari: शुङ्गा दशैते भोक्ष्यन्ति भूमिं वर्षशताधिकम् । ततः काण्वानियं भूमिर्यास्यत्यल्पगुणान्नृप ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: śuṅgā daśaite bhokṣyanti bhūmiṃ varṣaśatādhikam tataḥ kāṇvāniyaṃ bhūmiryāsyatyalpaguṇānnṛpa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ yā class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ yā class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ yā class 2 verb], [locative single from √ yā class 2 verb]

translation: His son will be Vasumitra who will beget Bhadraka who will have Pulinda as his son. His son will be Ghoṣa to whom will be born Vajramitra as the son.

devanagari: शुङ्गं हत्वा देवभूतिं काण्वोऽमात्यस्तु कामिनम् । स्वयं करिष्यते राज्यं वसुदेवो महामतिः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: śuṅgaṃ hatvā devabhūtiṃ kāṇvo svayaṃ kariṣyate rājyaṃ vasudevo mahāmatiḥ

[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ rāj ], [accusative single from √ rāj ]

translation: The next ruling prince will be Bhāgavata from, whom will be born Devabhūti as it is reported. These ten Śuṅga kings[10] will enjoy the kingship of the earth for a little over a hundred years.
purport:
Footnote [10]:
The list of Śuṅga kings in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa concurs fairly well with Pargiter’s Purāṇa text list. But the Bhāgavata Purāṇa states ten as the number of Śuṅga kings but actually names nine kings. SR however clarifies that Puṣyamitra, the commander-in-chief of Bṛhadratha Maurya, who was killed by Puṣyamitra should be regarded as the 1st Śuṅga king and thus makes the number of kings ten. Bhāvāratha Dīpikā states that the period of this dynasty was 112 years. Thus the Śuṅgas ruled from circa B.C. 187 to 75 B.C. This dynasty did not only stem the tide of Greek and other foreign invaders but rejuvenated Hinduism by performing an Aśvamedha sacrifice. The spread of Bhāgavatism under them is remarkable.—BVB. Age of Imp. Unity, pp 95-99

devanagari: तस्य पुत्रस्तु भूमित्रस्तस्य नारायणः सुतः । काण्वायना इमे भूमिं चत्वारिंशच्च पञ्च च । शतानि त्रीणि भोक्ष्यन्ति वर्षाणां च कलौ युगे ॥ १९...
english_devnagari: tasya putrastu bhūmitrastasya nārāyaṇaḥ sutaḥ kāṇvāyanā ime bhūmiṃ catvāriṃśacca pañca ca śatāni trīṇi bhokṣyanti varṣāṇāṃ ca kalau yuge
translation: Later on, O king Parīkṣit, the earth will pass under the control of the Kaṇva dynasty of every poor merit. Having killed the dissolute monarch Devabhūti Śuṅga (through a female slave), the highly intelligent Vasudeva himself will rule over the earth. His son will be Bhūmitra whose son will be Nārāyaṇa. Nārāyaṇa will have a famous son called Suśarmā.

devanagari: हत्वा काण्वं सुशर्माणं तद्भृत्यो वृषलो बली । गां भोक्ष्यत्यन्ध्रजातीयः कञ्चित्कालमसत्तमः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: hatvā kāṇvaṃ suśarmāṇaṃ tadbhṛtyo vṛṣalo balī gāṃ bhokṣyatyandhrajātīyaḥ kañcitkālamasattamaḥ
translation: Later on, O king Parīkṣit, the earth will pass under the control of the Kaṇva dynasty of every poor merit. Having killed the dissolute monarch Devabhūti Śuṅga (through a female slave), the highly intelligent Vasudeva himself will rule over the earth. His son will be Bhūmitra whose son will be Nārāyaṇa. Nārāyaṇa will have a famous son called Suśarmā.

devanagari: कृष्णनामाथ तद्भ्राता भविता पृथिवीपतिः । श्रीशान्तकर्णस्तत्पुत्रः पौर्णमासस्तु तत्सुतः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛṣṇanāmātha tadbhrātā bhavitā pṛthivīpatiḥ śrīśāntakarṇastatputraḥ paurṇamāsastu tatsutaḥ
translation: In this way, kings of the Kaṇva dynasty will enjoy rulership of the earth for forty-five years from 75 B.C. to 30 B.C. though the Purāṇic statement is three hundred forty-five years of the Kali age.

devanagari: लम्बोदरस्तु तत्पुत्रस्तस्माच्चिबिलको नृपः । मेघस्वातिश्चिबिलकादटमानस्तु तस्य च ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: lambodarastu tatputrastasmāccibilako nṛpaḥ meghasvātiścibilakādaṭamānastu tasya ca
translation: Murdering king Suśarmā of the Kaṇva dynasty, his low born servant Balī[11] of the Āndhra race, one of the wretched most persons, will rule over the earth for some time.
purport:
Footnote [11]:
The author of the Bh. P. does not know that his real name is Simuka and uses the adjective ‘Powerful’. As Pargiter’s Dynasties of the Kali Age (DKA) (P. 38 Notes 14, 16) shows, other Purāṇas use similar adjectives balīyas, balī with reference to Simuka. The consensus of Purāṇas on ‘Simuka’ as the name of this king is confirmed by numismatics and in Buhler’s List of Brāhmī Inscriptions. The author of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa seems to be a staunch orthodox Brāhmaṇa, so like Candragupta Maurya, Simuka or Balī is called Vṛṣala though his descendants like Gautamī-putra Śātakarṇi (in Nasik inscriptions) assume the title eka-Brāhmaṇa (the unique Brāhmaṇa). The prejudice against Āndhras as Dasyus dates from the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa. Simuka is called Rājā Simuka-Sātavāhana and as the legend goes Satavāhanas or Śālivāhanas were of mixed Brāhmaṇa and Nāga origins (Dvātriṃśat-Puttalikā). The epigraphic evidence of the associations of Sātavāhanas with Śakas and Nāgas shows that the orthodox Brāhmaṇas regarded them as vṛṣalas or degraded Brāhmaṇas or outcastes despite Sātavāhana’s claim to Brāhmaṇa-hood—The Age of Imp. Unity, pp. 106-107, etc.

devanagari: अनिष्टकर्मा हालेयस्तलकस्तस्य चात्मजः । पुरीषभीरुस्तत्पुत्रस्ततो राजा सुनन्दनः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: aniṣṭakarmā hāleyastalakastasya cātmajaḥ purīṣabhīrustatputrastato rājā sunandanaḥ
translation: After him his brother called Kṛṣṇa will be the next king. His son will be Śāntakarṇa and his son Paurṇamāsa.

devanagari: चकोरो बहवो यत्र शिवस्वातिररिन्दमः । तस्यापि गोमती पुत्रः पुरीमान्भविता ततः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: cakoro bahavo yatra śivasvātirarindamaḥ tasyāpi gomatī putraḥ purīmānbhavitā tataḥ
translation: Lambodara will be his son and from him will be born king Cibilaka. He will beget Meghasvāti whose son will be Aṭamāna.

devanagari: मेदशिराः शिवस्कन्दो यज्ञश्रीस्तत्सुतस्ततः । विजयस्तत्सुतो भाव्यश्चन्द्र विज्ञः सलोमधिः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: medaśirāḥ śivaskando yajñaśrīstatsutastataḥ vijayastatsuto bhāvyaścandra vijñaḥ salomadhiḥ
translation: Aniṣṭakarmā and Hāleya will then follow. Hāleya’s son will be Talaka. He will beget Purīṣabhīru who will be succeeded by his son king Sunandana.

devanagari: एते त्रिंशन्नृपतयश्चत्वार्यब्दशतानि च । षट्पञ्चाशच्च पृथिवीं भोक्ष्यन्ति कुरुनन्दन ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: ete triṃśannṛpatayaścatvāryabdaśatāni ca ṣaṭpañcāśacca pṛthivīṃ bhokṣyanti kurunandana
translation: Sunandana will have a son called Cakora who will be succeeded by eight sons known as Bahus collectively, the youngest of them being Śivasvāti, the vanquisher of enemies. He will beget a son called Gomatī-putra whose son will be Purīman,

devanagari: सप्ताभीरा आवभृत्या दश गर्दभिनो नृपाः । कङ्काः षोडश भूपाला भविष्यन्त्यतिलोलुपाः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: saptābhīrā āvabhṛtyā daśa gardabhino nṛpāḥ kaṅkāḥ ṣoḍaśa bhūpālā bhaviṣyantyatilolupāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual], [nominative plural], [vocative dual], [vocative plural], [accusative dual], [accusative plural]

translation: The succession of princes after Purīman will be Medaḥśiras, Śivaskanda, his son Yajñaśrī and his prince Vijaya and his successors Candravijña and Salomadhi.

devanagari: ततोऽष्टौ यवना भाव्याश्चतुर्दश तुरुष्ककाः । भूयो दश गुरुण्डाश्च मौला एकादशैव तु ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: tato bhūyo daśa guruṇḍāśca maulā ekādaśaiva tu

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ aś class 5 verb], [vocative dual from √ aś class 5 verb], [accusative dual from √ aś class 5 verb]
[nominative plural from √ bhū ], [vocative plural from √ bhū ]
[nominative plural from √ bhū ], [vocative plural from √ bhū ], [accusative plural from √ bhū ]

translation: These thirty kings[12] will enjoy the sovereignty of the world for four hundred and fifty six years, O delight of the Kuru race.
purport:
Footnote [12]:
The Bhāgavata Purāṇa actually enumerates 23 kings though in conformity with other Purāṇas, the number of kings is stated as thirty. Pargiter’s single unified list of Purāṇa Text (DKA pp. 38-43, 71-72) and his co-ordinated list (DK.A- 36) show differences in names of the kings. The list of 30 kings as given in Matsya P. is quoted in The Age of Imp. Unity, pp, 706-707.
Viṣṇu P. (4.24.5) correctly calls these kings Āndhrabhṛtyas, for after the Andhras probably (as happened in the case of Śuṅgas and Kaṇvas) the servants of the Āndhra rulers became the rulers and came to be known as such.

devanagari: एते भोक्ष्यन्ति पृथिवीं दश वर्षशतानि च । नवाधिकां च नवतिं मौला एकादश क्षितिम् ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: ete bhokṣyanti pṛthivīṃ daśa varṣaśatāni ca navādhikāṃ ca navatiṃ maulā ekādaśa kṣitim
translation: At their capital Avabhṛti[13], seven Ābhīra princes will rule the earth, ten kings of Gardhabhī dynasty and sixteen of the Kaṅka (Śaka) race—all of them very greedy and lewd will hold the sway.
purport:
Footnote [13]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā is simply offering a conjecture when he explains ‘Āvabhṛtyas,’ as the rulers of Avabhṛti.

devanagari: भोक्ष्यन्त्यब्दशतान्यङ्ग त्रीणि तैः संस्थिते ततः । किलकिलायां नृपतयो भूतनन्दोऽथ वङ्गिरिः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: bhokṣyantyabdaśatānyaṅga trīṇi taiḥ saṃsthite tataḥ kilakilāyāṃ nṛpatayo bhūtanando
translation: They will be succeeded by eight Yavana rulers (Bactrian Greeks), and fourteen kings of the Turuṣka race. Again there will be ten kings of Guruṇḍa (Probably Maruṇḍas vide DKA P.45) race and eleven of the Mauna (Hūṇa) dynasty.

devanagari: शिशुनन्दिश्च तद्भ्राता यशोनन्दिः प्रवीरकः । इत्येते वै वर्षशतं भविष्यन्त्यधिकानि षट् ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: śiśunandiśca tadbhrātā yaśonandiḥ pravīrakaḥ ityete vai varṣaśataṃ bhaviṣyantyadhikāni ṣaṭ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [locative single from √ i class 2 verb]
[nominative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative dual from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative dual from √ i class 2 verb]
[present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]
[nominative dual], [nominative plural], [vocative dual], [vocative plural], [accusative dual], [accusative plural]

translation: These kings (beginning from Ābhīras upto Maunas, viz. sixty-five kings will enjoy the sovereignty of the earth for (a total of) one thousand ninety-nine years[14] And the eleven Mauna (Hūṇa) kings will rule over the earth for three hundred years, O king. When their dynasty ended, at the capital town of Kilikila, kings Bhūtananda, Vaṅgiri Śiśunandi and his famous warrior brother Yaśanandi will reign for one hundred and six years.[15]
purport:
Footnote [14]:
The above four verses, viz. 29-33 mention some local dynasties. References to other Purāṇas and Pargiter’s DKA (Dynasty of the Kali Age) show that some of them were contemporaries. Hence the duration of these dynasties viz. 1099 years as given in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is not correct. In fact, the author of this chapter of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is less critical and relics on hearsay evidence. Some of these are foreign tribes viz. Śakas (called Kaṅkas in the Bh. P.) and Hūṇas (called Maunas here).

Footnote [15]:
Compare DKA: Dynasties of 3rd Cent. A.D.—pp. 50-53.

devanagari: तेषां त्रयोदश सुता भवितारश्च बाह्लिकाः । पुष्पमित्रोऽथ राजन्यो दुर्मित्रोऽस्य तथैव च ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: teṣāṃ trayodaśa sutā bhavitāraśca bāhlikāḥ puṣpamitro

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ su class 5 verb], [vocative plural from √ su class 5 verb]

When their dynasty ended, at the capital town of Kilikila, kings Bhūtananda, Vaṅgiri Śiśunandi and his famous warrior brother Yaśanandi will reign for one hundred and six years.[15]

Footnote [14]:
The above four verses, viz. 29-33 mention some local dynasties. References to other Purāṇas and Pargiter’s DKA (Dynasty of the Kali Age) show that some of them were contemporaries. Hence the duration of these dynasties viz. 1099 years as given in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is not correct. In fact, the author of this chapter of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is less critical and relics on hearsay evidence. Some of these are foreign tribes viz. Śakas (called Kaṅkas in the Bh. P.) and Hūṇas (called Maunas here).

Footnote [15]:
Compare DKA: Dynasties of 3rd Cent. A.D.—pp. 50-53.

devanagari: एककाला इमे भूपाः सप्तान्ध्राः सप्त कौशलाः । विदूरपतयो भाव्या निषधास्तत एव हि ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: ekakālā ime bhūpāḥ saptāndhrāḥ sapta kauśalāḥ vidūrapatayo bhāvyā niṣadhāstata eva hi

[nominative plural from √ bhū ], [vocative plural from √ bhū ], [accusative plural from √ bhū ]

translation: These kings (beginning from Ābhīras upto Maunas, viz. sixty-five kings will enjoy the sovereignty of the earth for (a total of) one thousand ninety-nine years[14] And the eleven Mauna (Hūṇa) kings will rule over the earth for three hundred years, O king. When their dynasty ended, at the capital town of Kilikila, kings Bhūtananda, Vaṅgiri Śiśunandi and his famous warrior brother Yaśanandi will reign for one hundred and six years.[15]

Footnote [14]:
The above four verses, viz. 29-33 mention some local dynasties. References to other Purāṇas and Pargiter’s DKA (Dynasty of the Kali Age) show that some of them were contemporaries. Hence the duration of these dynasties viz. 1099 years as given in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is not correct. In fact, the author of this chapter of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa is less critical and relics on hearsay evidence. Some of these are foreign tribes viz. Śakas (called Kaṅkas in the Bh. P.) and Hūṇas (called Maunas here).

Footnote [15]:
Compare DKA: Dynasties of 3rd Cent. A.D.—pp. 50-53.

devanagari: मागधानां तु भविता विश्वस्फूर्जिः पुरञ्जयः । करिष्यत्यपरो वर्णान्पुलिन्दयदुमद्र कान् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: māgadhānāṃ tu bhavitā viśvasphūrjiḥ purañjayaḥ kariṣyatyaparo varṇānpulindayadumadra kān

[nominative dual from √ kṛ class 3 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṛ class 3 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṛ class 3 verb], [locative single from √ kṛ class 3 verb], [nominative dual from √ kṛ class 6 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṛ class 6 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṛ class 6 verb], [locative single from √ kṛ class 6 verb]

[nominative dual from √ kṝ class 5 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṝ class 5 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṝ class 5 verb], [locative single from √ kṝ class 5 verb], [nominative dual from √ kṝ class 9 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṝ class 9 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṝ class 9 verb], [locative single from √ kṝ class 9 verb]

translation: They will have thirteen sons called Bāhlīkas. Then will rule the Kṣatriya Prince Puṣyamitra and his son Dumitra.

devanagari: प्रजाश्चाब्रह्मभूयिष्ठाः स्थापयिष्यति दुर्मतिः । वीर्यवान्क्षत्रमुत्साद्य पद्मवत्यां स वै पुरि । अनुगङ्गमाप्रयागं गुप्तां भोक्ष्यति मेद...
english_devnagari: prajāścābrahmabhūyiṣṭhāḥ sthāpayiṣyati durmatiḥ vīryavānkṣatramutsādya padmavatyāṃ sa vai puri anugaṅgamāprayāgaṃ guptāṃ bhokṣyati medinīm

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: All these will be contemporary kings ruling over different states. Seven of these will be the rulers of Āndhra and seven kings of Kosai. The kings of Viḍūra and Niṣadha also will be out of these Bālhīkas.

devanagari: सौराष्ट्रावन्त्याभीराश्च शूरा अर्बुदमालवाः । व्रात्या द्विजा भविष्यन्ति शूद्र प्राया जनाधिपाः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: saurāṣṭrāvantyābhīrāśca śūrā arbudamālavāḥ vrātyā dvijā bhaviṣyanti śūdra prāyā janādhipāḥ
translation: There will be a king of Magadhas called Viśvasphūrji. He will be well known as Purañjaya the second. He will reduce the higher castes to the status of Pulindas, Yadus and Madrakas.[16]
purport:
Footnote [16]:
BVB. AGE Imp. Unity regards Viśvasphūrji as a foreign ruler of Magadha, p. 177 Ft. Note No. 3.
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā gives Viśvasphūrji the credit of creating the subcastes like Pulindas.

devanagari: सिन्धोस्तटं चन्द्रभागां कौन्तीं काश्मीरमण्डलम् । भोक्ष्यन्ति शूद्रा व्रात्याद्या म्लेच्छाश्चाब्रह्मवर्चसः ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: sindhostaṭaṃ candrabhāgāṃ kauntīṃ kāśmīramaṇḍalam bhokṣyanti śūdrā vrātyādyā mlecchāścābrahmavarcasaḥ
translation: That powerful but wicked-minded prince will establish a caste-less society where there will be no reverence to Vedas and Brāhmaṇas. He will exterminate the Kṣatriyas as well. In his capital city of Padmāvatī, he will enjoy a well- guarded state from Haraḍwar to Prayāga in the valley of the Gaṅgā.

devanagari: तुल्यकाला इमे राजन्म्लेच्छप्रायाश्च भूभृतः । एतेऽधर्मानृतपराः फल्गुदास्तीव्रमन्यवः ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: tulyakālā ime rājanmlecchaprāyāśca bhūbhṛtaḥ ete

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ rāj class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ rāj class 1 verb]

translation: The twice-born castes of Saurāṣṭra, Avanti, Ābhīra, Śūra, Arbuda and Mālava countries will become fallen (due to lapse or non-performance of saṃskāras, such as the investiture of the sacred thread) and the rulers of these people will be mostly of Śūdra castes.

devanagari: स्त्रीबालगोद्विजघ्नाश्च परदारधनादृताः । उदितास्तमितप्राया अल्पसत्त्वाल्पकायुषः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: strībālagodvijaghnāśca paradāradhanādṛtāḥ uditāstamitaprāyā alpasattvālpakāyuṣaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vad class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ vad class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ vad class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ vad class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ vad class 1 verb]

translation: Śūdras, persons fallen from higher castes, Mlecchas all bereft of Vedic culture and way of life—will be the rulers over the banks of the Sindhu and the Candrabhāgā (the Chinab), the city of Kaunti and the region of Kaśmīra.

devanagari: असंस्कृताः क्रियाहीना रजसा तमसावृताः । प्रजास्ते भक्षयिष्यन्ति म्लेच्छा राजन्यरूपिणः ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: asaṃskṛtāḥ kriyāhīnā rajasā tamasāvṛtāḥ prajāste bhakṣayiṣyanti mlecchā rājanyarūpiṇaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ bhakṣ ], [vocative plural from √ bhakṣ ], [accusative plural from √ bhakṣ ]

translation: O Parīkṣit! All these contemporary kings will be as good as Mlecchas, bent on unrighteousness, falsehood, miserly and ferocious.

devanagari: तन्नाथास्ते जनपदास्तच्छीलाचारवादिनः । अन्योन्यतो राजभिश्च क्षयं यास्यन्ति पीडिताः ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: tannāthāste janapadāstacchīlācāravādinaḥ anyonyato rājabhiśca kṣayaṃ yāsyanti pīḍitāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ yā class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ yā class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ yā class 2 verb]
[nominative plural from √ pīḍ class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ pīḍ class 10 verb]
[nominative plural from √ pīḍ class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ pīḍ class 10 verb], [accusative plural from √ pīḍ class 10 verb]

translation: (With no compunctious) they will murder women, children, Brāhmaṇas, slaughter cows. They will covet after the women and property of others. Subject to vicissitudes of fate and overwhelmed with joy or grief, they will be poor in strength and of short duration of life.
